metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = converging / accurate region, CRIMSON = diverging / error blowup
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no complex-analysis proof of zero radius of convergence, no Borel resummation, no QED fine-structure application, no Bender-Wu coefficients formula

beats.narration_text: A student runs ten perturbative corrections to a quartic oscillator. Each term is smaller than the last. Looks like convergence.
Term eleven. Instead of shrinking again, the correction explodes — and keeps exploding. Everything past term ten is worse than term ten.
A series whose terms keep shrinking should converge. Here every term shrank for ten orders — then the whole thing blew up. Why?

props.headline: A series whose terms keep shrinking should converge. Here every term…

card.headline: A series that shrinks should converge.
card.dek: Here every term shrank for ten orders — then blew up. Why?

beats.narration_text: Perturbation theory expands the energy as a power series in a coupling constant. Smaller coupling means smaller terms — that's the whole promise.
If ten terms all shrink, a mathematician would say: evidence of convergence. Keep computing more terms and the answer improves forever.
A series converges when its partial sums close in on a fixed target. The terms shrinking is a necessary sign — but not sufficient proof.

props.headline: A series converges when its partial sums close in on a…

beats.narration_text: Here's the catch. Flip the coupling negative — make the quartic term minus lambda instead of plus. The potential now slopes down to negative infinity.
For any negative lambda, however small, there is no ground state. The particle escapes to infinity. The system simply does not exist.
That means the energy has a singularity on the negative coupling axis. Any Taylor series centered at zero can't reach that singularity — its radius of convergence is zero.
The series coefficients grow like a factorial — k-factorial. That factorial growth eventually overtakes any fixed power of lambda, no matter how small.
So the terms first shrink because lambda-to-the-k gets small, then blow up because k-factorial gets huge. There is a crossover — and that crossover is the key.
Plot the cumulative error against truncation order. It descends to a minimum at N-star — the optimal truncation — then climbs steeply. Stop at N-star.
At N-star the error is exponentially small — of order e to the minus constant over lambda. Invisible to every finite-order expansion, but real.

props.headline: At N-star the error is exponentially small — of order e…

beats.narration_text: Dyson made this argument in 1952 for quantum electrodynamics. Flip the sign of the fine-structure constant and the vacuum destabilizes. The QED series also has zero radius of convergence.

props.headline: Dyson made this argument in 1952 for quantum electrodynamics. Flip the…

beats.narration_text: Yet QED is the most accurate physical theory ever tested. Its perturbative predictions match experiment to twelve decimal places — because they stop at optimal order.

Illustrative: a student computes eight corrections for coupling 0.05. The terms read 0.82, 0.94, 0.975, 0.990, 0.996, 0.998, 0.9985, 0.9986. Then term nine: 1.002. Ten: 1.015. Eleven: 1.07. Twelve: 1.4. Optimal truncation was term eight.
A divergent series can give the right answer — if you stop at the optimal term. Quantum mechanics.

props.headline: A divergent series can give the right answer — if you…

card.headline: Stop at the optimal term.
card.dek: A divergent series gives the right answer — if you know when to stop.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Approximation Methods and Real Systems series.
